Yet within the oppression, Black cultural creativity flourished — jazz, blues, and the unique New Orleans musical culture continued to develop.
1890s.daily_life_snapshot: In New Orleans in the 1890s, the sounds of brass bands, ragtime pianos, and blues guitar emanated from the neighborhoods of Treme, Storyville, and uptown. Streetcars ran through segregated streets. Markets sold crawfish, shrimp, and oysters. In the sugar parishes, Black families sharecropped on the same land their parents had been enslaved on, paying rent in labor and crop shares. In the prairie country, rice farmers — now including some Midwesterners who brought mechanical farming techniques — transformed the landscape. Cajun communities held bals de maison (house dances) and spoke French exclusively.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Plessy v. Ferguson legalizes segregation nationally (1896)
1898 constitutional convention disenfranchises virtually all Black voters
Lynching of eleven Italians in New Orleans (March 1891)
Hurricane devastates Cheniere Caminada — over 700 killed (October 1893)
Panic of 1893 economic depression
Yellow fever (declining but still present)
narrative_hooks: Homer Plessy waits in a New Orleans courtroom as the Supreme Court rules against him — 'separate but equal' will define American race relations for sixty years
A Sicilian immigrant in New Orleans reads about the lynching of his countrymen and understands that in America, being white enough to survive is not guaranteed for everyone who looks like him

A Creole of color whose family has been free and educated for four generations is told he cannot vote under the new constitution — the grandfather clause does not apply to his grandfather, who was free before the law cared about freedom
A fisherman on Cheniere Caminada watches the barometer drop and knows the storm is coming, but there is nowhere to go

key_events: Jennings oil field discovery (September 21, 1901) — launches Louisiana's oil industry
Robert Charles riot in New Orleans — Black man fights back against police, triggering a citywide race riot (July 24–27, 1900)
Storyville, New Orleans's legalized red-light district, operates from 1897–1917 — incubates jazz
Yellow fever's last major U.S. epidemic strikes New Orleans (1905) — 452 die; mosquito-control campaigns follow
Oil exploration expands across southern Louisiana — the state's economic future begins to shift
Boll weevil reaches Louisiana, devastating cotton crops (arriving early 1900s)
1900s.economic_conditions: The Jennings oil strike in 1901 signaled a new economic era for Louisiana. Oil exploration and drilling expanded rapidly across the southern part of the state, creating jobs and attracting investment. Meanwhile, the boll weevil devastated cotton, forcing agricultural diversification. Sugar struggled with foreign competition but survived through tariff protection. Rice farming prospered. Timber companies continued stripping Louisiana's forests at an unsustainable rate. New Orleans's port remained important but faced increasing competition from Gulf ports in Texas.
1900s.migration_patterns: The oil boom attracted workers from across the South. Black Louisianians continued migrating north — the Robert Charles incident symbolized the violence that drove the exodus. Italian immigration to New Orleans continued. Cajun communities began experiencing outside contact through oil exploration in their bayou country. Timber workers followed the logging frontier through Louisiana's piney woods. The decline of cotton pushed some farmers off the land.
1900s.social_context: The Robert Charles incident (1900) demonstrated that Black resistance to white supremacy existed, even if it was met with overwhelming force — mobs rampaged through Black neighborhoods for days. Storyville, while primarily a story of exploitation and vice, was also the incubator of jazz — Black musicians like Buddy Bolden, Jelly Roll Morton, and Joe 'King' Oliver played in its venues. The 1905 yellow fever epidemic was the last major outbreak, thanks to the discovery that mosquitoes were the vector. Jim Crow was firmly entrenched, but Black cultural creativity — in music, food, faith, and community organizations — flourished despite oppression.
1900s.daily_life_snapshot: In New Orleans, Buddy Bolden's cornet could be heard across the city on a quiet night — jazz was becoming the soundtrack of a new century. The French Market, Congo Square (now Beauregard Square), and the levee remained gathering places. Oil derricks appeared in rice paddies and sugar fields, their arrival transforming the economy of communities that had known nothing but agriculture. In the bayous, Cajun trappers suddenly found oil men offering to lease their swampland. The last yellow fever epidemic (1905) prompted public health reforms that made New Orleans somewhat safer.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Robert Charles race riot in New Orleans (July 1900) — dozens of Black residents killed
Last major yellow fever epidemic (1905)
Boll weevil devastates cotton crops
Continued Jim Crow oppression and racial violence
Environmental destruction from logging and early oil drilling
narrative_hooks: Buddy Bolden lifts his cornet on a Saturday night in Storyville and plays a sound no one has heard before — it will change American music forever
A Cajun trapper stares at an oil derrick rising from the swamp behind his house and wonders what black gold means for people who have always lived by the water
Robert Charles loads his rifle in a New Orleans boarding house, knowing he will not survive the day but refusing to die without resistance
A doctor in New Orleans watches mosquitoes being systematically eradicated and dares to believe the yellow fever nightmare may finally end

key_events: Storyville shut down by the U.S. Navy, ending New Orleans's legalized red-light district (November 12, 1917)
Jazz musicians leave New Orleans for Chicago, Kansas City, and New York — the music goes national
World War I (1917–1918) — Louisiana sends troops; military training camps open
Great Mississippi River flood threatens but does not overwhelm New Orleans (1912, foretelling 1927)
Oil production increases; refineries begin operating in Louisiana
Lumber companies exhaust Louisiana's virgin forests — cutover lands left behind
1910s.economic_conditions: Oil became increasingly important to Louisiana's economy, with drilling expanding across the southern parishes and into the Gulf Coast marshes. Sugar production continued but faced labor shortages as workers left for better-paying oil and war-industry jobs. Cotton declined further under boll weevil pressure. The timber industry began declining as virgin forests were exhausted — lumber companies left behind cutover wasteland. World War I brought military spending and demand for petroleum products, accelerating the oil industry's growth. New Orleans's economy diversified modestly.
1910s.migration_patterns: The Great Migration accelerated dramatically during WWI — Black Louisianians moved to Chicago, Detroit, and other Northern cities in large numbers, drawn by factory jobs and fleeing Jim Crow. The closure of Storyville scattered New Orleans's jazz musicians northward, spreading the music nationally. Cajun men served in the military, many encountering the English-speaking world for the first time. Oil workers migrated into Louisiana from Texas and other states. Rural depopulation continued as agriculture struggled.
1910s.social_context: World War I exposed Louisiana's Cajun soldiers to intense pressure to abandon French and speak English — some were punished for speaking their mother tongue in the military. The closure of Storyville represented a moral reform movement but also displaced a functioning (if exploitative) community. Jazz went from a local New Orleans phenomenon to a national cultural force as musicians migrated north. Jim Crow continued unchallenged; Black Louisianians served in a segregated military. The timber industry's devastation of Louisiana's forests had environmental consequences that were only beginning to be understood.
1910s.daily_life_snapshot: In New Orleans, the closure of Storyville sent jazz musicians into the broader city and beyond. The French Quarter was aging and somewhat run-down, its future as a tourist destination not yet apparent. In the oil fields, roughnecks worked twelve-hour shifts on wooden derricks, risking blowouts and fires. In the sugar parishes, grinding season still dictated the annual rhythm. Cajun communities maintained their French-language, Catholic culture but faced increasing pressure to assimilate. In the piney woods, lumber company departures left behind stump-covered wastelands and unemployed workers.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: World War I casualties among Louisiana servicemen
Influenza pandemic (1918–1919) hits Louisiana hard
Deforestation leaves cutover wasteland across northern and central Louisiana
Boll weevil continues devastating cotton
Jim Crow oppression and racial violence unabated
narrative_hooks: A New Orleans jazz musician packs his cornet and boards a train for Chicago after Storyville closes — he is carrying Louisiana's greatest cultural gift to the world
A Cajun soldier in the trenches of France is ordered to stop speaking French — the only language he has ever known
An oil field roughneck watches a geyser of crude oil erupt from a Louisiana marsh and sees a fortune — or a disaster
A Black family boards the Illinois Central for Chicago, leaving Louisiana forever, carrying nothing but a suitcase and the memory of home

key_events: Oil production booms — Louisiana becomes a major petroleum-producing state
Huey Long elected governor on a populist platform (1928) — promises roads, bridges, free textbooks, and war on Standard Oil
Great Mississippi Flood of 1927 — the most destructive river flood in U.S. history devastates Louisiana
New Orleans city leaders dynamite the Caernarvon levee, deliberately flooding St. Bernard and Plaquemines parishes to save the city (April 29, 1927)
Ku Klux Klan gains political power in northern Louisiana (early 1920s)
Prohibition and jazz define New Orleans's cultural reputation nationally
1920s.economic_conditions: Oil transformed Louisiana's economy in the 1920s, making it one of the nation's leading petroleum producers. Standard Oil's Baton Rouge refinery became one of the world's largest. The oil industry created jobs, attracted investment, and generated tax revenue — and also created Huey Long's political issue (he campaigned on taxing oil companies to fund public services). Agriculture remained important but secondary. The 1927 flood devastated agriculture in the Delta region. Sugar and rice survived; cotton continued declining. New Orleans's economy benefited from oil-related shipping and refining.
1920s.migration_patterns: The Great Migration continued drawing Black Louisianians northward — the 1927 flood accelerated this movement, as displaced Black farmers who had been living in debt peonage fled to Northern cities. The flood particularly devastated Black communities in the Delta parishes. Oil industry growth attracted workers to southern Louisiana. New Orleans's population grew modestly. Cajun communities began their long struggle between assimilation and cultural preservation — English-language education was mandatory, but French was spoken at home.
1920s.social_context: The 1927 flood was a turning point — it revealed that New Orleans's leaders would sacrifice poor, Black, and Cajun communities to protect the city (the Caernarvon dynamiting), it destroyed the plantation system's last hold on the Delta, and it accelerated the Great Migration. Huey Long's rise represented a populist revolt against the planter-oil company establishment — his free textbooks, road-building, and attacks on corporations were genuinely popular among poor white and (to a lesser extent) Black Louisianians. But Long was also autocratic and corrupt. The Klan gained power in northern Louisiana, targeting Catholics and Black citizens.
1920s.daily_life_snapshot: In New Orleans, jazz clubs thrived despite Prohibition (which was barely enforced in the city). Mardi Gras became increasingly elaborate and tourist-oriented. In the oil fields of south Louisiana, roughnecks and roustabouts worked dangerous jobs but earned good wages. In the flooded Delta parishes in 1927, families clung to levee tops or rooftops, waiting for rescue — Black refugees were held in camps under armed guard and forced to work on levee repairs. In Cajun country, the annual rhythm of trapping, fishing, and rice farming continued, but oil derricks dotted the landscape.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Great Mississippi Flood of 1927 — 700,000 displaced, massive devastation
Deliberate dynamiting of Caernarvon levee floods St. Bernard and Plaquemines parishes (April 1927)
Black flood refugees held in camps under armed guard, forced to labor
Ku Klux Klan violence and political influence (northern Louisiana)
Continuing Jim Crow oppression and racial violence
narrative_hooks: A Black farmer in the Delta clings to a levee top as the 1927 flood destroys everything he owns — then National Guardsmen force him to sandbag at gunpoint instead of letting him leave
Huey Long stands on a flatbed truck in rural Louisiana, promising free textbooks and paved roads to people who have never had either — and they believe him
A Cajun trapper in Plaquemines Parish watches the Caernarvon levee explode and his land disappear under water, sacrificed to save New Orleans
A jazz musician in a New Orleans speakeasy plays through Prohibition, in a city that never really bothered to go dry

key_events: Huey Long dominates Louisiana politics — builds roads, bridges, hospitals, LSU, and a political machine (1928–1935)
Huey Long elected U.S. Senator (1930) while retaining control of Louisiana state government
Long assassinated in the Louisiana State Capitol (September 8, 1935)
Long political machine continues under successors despite scandals
Great Depression devastates Louisiana's agricultural economy
Oil production provides some economic cushion; natural gas industry emerges
Bonnie and Clyde killed in an ambush in Bienville Parish (May 23, 1934)
1930s.economic_conditions: The Great Depression hit Louisiana's agricultural sector hard — sugar and cotton prices collapsed, and subsistence-level farmers faced starvation. Oil production provided a partial cushion, as Louisiana's petroleum industry was now a major national force. Huey Long's massive public works programs — funded by oil taxes — put thousands to work building roads, bridges, and buildings (including a new State Capitol). Federal New Deal programs supplemented Long's state initiatives. The fishing and trapping industries in the bayous provided subsistence for Cajun families. Natural gas emerged as a new extractive industry.
dominant_industries: public works construction (Long's programs and New Deal)
1930s.migration_patterns: The Depression and continued Jim Crow pushed Black Louisianians northward. Rural-to-urban migration within Louisiana continued. Oil industry towns grew. Cajun communities remained relatively stable but increasingly exposed to American culture through Long's road-building, which connected isolated bayou communities to the wider state. Some displaced sharecroppers drifted to towns and cities. The CCC and WPA employed young men in conservation and construction projects throughout the state.
1930s.social_context: Huey Long's legacy is Louisiana's most debated historical question. He genuinely improved the lives of poor Louisianians — building roads in a state that had almost none, providing free textbooks, funding public hospitals, and creating LSU as a major university. But he was also a demagogue who concentrated power, crushed opposition, and built a political machine that would corrupt Louisiana politics for decades. His assassination in 1935 shocked the nation. Jim Crow continued unabated — Long did little for Black Louisianians specifically, though some of his programs benefited them indirectly. Cajun communities experienced cultural pressure as English-language education and road connections opened their world.
1930s.daily_life_snapshot: Huey Long's roads transformed daily life — farmers who had never left their parishes could now drive to town. Free textbooks meant children from poor families could attend school. New public hospitals served communities that had never had a doctor. But the Depression meant many families still struggled: sharecroppers worked for a share of crops that were worth almost nothing. In the oil fields, workers earned relatively good wages but faced dangerous conditions. In New Orleans, WPA projects employed artists, musicians, and writers, documenting Louisiana's rich cultural heritage. Mardi Gras continued even during the Depression.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Great Depression devastates agricultural economy
Assassination of Huey Long (September 8, 1935)
Political corruption under Long machine
Continued Jim Crow oppression and racial violence
Hurricanes and flooding in coastal and river parishes
narrative_hooks: A sharecropper's child opens a free textbook for the first time, courtesy of Huey Long — and begins to imagine a life beyond the cotton field
Huey Long walks through the State Capitol he built, the most powerful man in Louisiana, moments before Dr. Carl Weiss steps out of the shadows
A Cajun fisherman drives his truck on a newly paved road to Baton Rouge and encounters a world where no one speaks French
A Black woman in rural Louisiana uses a WPA-built clinic for the first time — Huey's roads led her there, even if Huey never cared about people who look like her

key_events: World War II transforms Louisiana's economy — shipbuilding, military bases, and petroleum production surge
Higgins Industries in New Orleans builds over 20,000 landing craft (LCVP — 'Higgins Boats') used at D-Day and across the Pacific
Military bases expand: Fort Polk, Barksdale Field, Camp Beauregard, Camp Claiborne
Louisiana sends hundreds of thousands into military service
Oil and gas production accelerates, including offshore exploration
GI Bill provides opportunities for returning veterans, though discrimination limits Black access
Earl Long (Huey's brother) serves as governor, continuing populist politics (1939–1940, 1948–1952)
1940s.economic_conditions: World War II ended the Depression in Louisiana and established the petroleum-industrial complex that would define the state's economy for decades. Higgins Industries' production of landing craft was a pivotal contribution to the Allied war effort and made New Orleans a major shipbuilding center. Military bases pumped money into surrounding communities. Oil and gas production boomed, with the first offshore drilling platforms appearing in the late 1940s. Petrochemical plants began lining the Mississippi River between Baton Rouge and New Orleans — the beginning of 'Cancer Alley.' Agriculture recovered with wartime demand for sugar, rice, and other products.
1940s.migration_patterns: The war drew thousands of Louisianians into military service and defense industry jobs. Many Black Louisianians who served or worked in Northern factories during the war did not return. Cajun soldiers encountered the wider world and brought back new ideas and English-language fluency. Military bases attracted families from across the country. The postwar period saw continued Black out-migration to Northern cities. Oil industry expansion drew workers to southern Louisiana. Offshore drilling created a new commuter pattern — workers spent weeks on platforms and weeks at home.
1940s.social_context: The war brought profound changes. Higgins Industries was notably integrated for its time — Black and white workers built landing craft side by side, though in segregated sections of the factory. Cajun soldiers who had been punished for speaking French in the 1910s now fought for France's liberation. Black veterans returned to Louisiana expecting equality and found Jim Crow intact. The GI Bill offered transformative opportunities but was administered discriminatorily, limiting Black veterans' access to education and home loans. Earl Long's populism included more explicit support for Black rights than his brother Huey's had — he would become famous for defending Black suffrage.
1940s.daily_life_snapshot: During the war, New Orleans was a boomtown — shipyard workers filled the streets, military personnel crowded the French Quarter, and the economy hummed with wartime production. Rationing affected sugar (ironic in sugar-producing Louisiana), gasoline, and meat. Women entered the workforce in unprecedented numbers. In the oil fields, production ran around the clock. After the war, returning veterans used GI Bill benefits to attend LSU, Tulane, and other universities. Mardi Gras returned to full scale after wartime curtailment. The petrochemical corridor began transforming the landscape between Baton Rouge and New Orleans.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: World War II casualties among Louisiana servicemen
Racial discrimination in defense industries and GI Bill administration
Hurricanes affecting Gulf Coast (1947 hurricane caused major damage)
Beginning of petrochemical pollution — 'Cancer Alley' takes shape
Continued Jim Crow oppression
narrative_hooks: A Higgins Industries worker rivets a landing craft hull in New Orleans, knowing the boat he is building will carry men onto a hostile beach — possibly including his own son
A Cajun soldier wading ashore on D-Day in a Higgins Boat built in his home state liberates a French village where people speak a language he understands
A Black veteran returns to New Orleans with a Bronze Star and is told he cannot use the GI Bill to buy a house in a white neighborhood
A fisherman in Plaquemines Parish watches the first offshore drilling platform rise from the Gulf and wonders what it means for his shrimp grounds

key_events: Brown v. Board of Education (1954) — Louisiana's legislature passes interposition resolutions and prepares to resist desegregation
Oil and petrochemical industry expansion transforms Louisiana's Gulf Coast and Mississippi River corridor
Governor Earl Long champions voter registration for Black citizens, is committed to a mental institution by political enemies (1959)

New Orleans school desegregation crisis begins — Ruby Bridges and three other Black girls integrate William Frantz and McDonogh No. 19 elementary schools (November 14, 1960, building from 1950s legal battles)
Offshore oil drilling expands into the Gulf of Mexico — Louisiana becomes the center of the U.S. offshore industry
Interstate Highway system construction begins in Louisiana
1950s.economic_conditions: The 1950s cemented Louisiana's identity as a petroleum state. Oil and gas production — both onshore and in the rapidly expanding offshore sector — drove the economy. Petrochemical plants multiplied along the Mississippi River corridor, providing well-paying industrial jobs but also environmental devastation. Sugar and rice remained important but secondary. Tourism grew, particularly in New Orleans, where the French Quarter's renovation attracted visitors. The fishing and seafood industry remained vital to coastal communities. Louisiana remained a poor state overall, with wealth concentrated in the oil industry and its supporting sectors.
1950s.migration_patterns: Black out-migration continued, though at a somewhat reduced rate. The oil and petrochemical industry attracted workers from across the South and beyond. Cajun communities experienced rapid cultural change as television, highways, and English-language education eroded French language and traditional ways. Suburban growth around Baton Rouge, New Orleans, and Lafayette reflected national patterns. Offshore oil work created a commuter culture unique to Louisiana — workers flew to platforms for weeks at a time.
1950s.social_context: Louisiana's response to Brown v. Board was fierce resistance. The state legislature passed interposition resolutions, and political leaders promised to maintain segregation. Earl Long, the most colorful governor in Louisiana's colorful political history, broke with white supremacist orthodoxy by defending Black voter registration — his enemies had him committed to an asylum. The New Orleans school integration crisis of 1960 (building from 1950s court orders) produced iconic images of hatred: white mobs screaming at six-year-old Ruby Bridges. Cajun cultural revival movements began to push back against decades of forced Anglicization.
1950s.daily_life_snapshot: In New Orleans, the French Quarter was transforming from a run-down Creole neighborhood into a tourist destination and bohemian enclave. Bourbon Street was becoming famous (and infamous). Offshore oil workers commuted by helicopter and boat to platforms in the Gulf, living a strange two-world existence. In Cajun country, the traditional fais do-do was being replaced by Saturday night dances at dance halls, but the music — Cajun French with accordion and fiddle — endured and was evolving into zydeco under Black Creole influence. In Black communities, churches served as organizing centers for the coming civil rights struggle.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Massive resistance to school desegregation
Hurricane Audrey devastates Cameron Parish — 416–526 killed (June 27, 1957)
Environmental pollution from petrochemical industry — 'Cancer Alley' expands
Governor Earl Long committed to asylum by political enemies (1959)
Continued Jim Crow oppression and racial violence
narrative_hooks: Six-year-old Ruby Bridges walks through a screaming white mob into William Frantz Elementary School, escorted by federal marshals, and America confronts the reality of its racism
Earl Long stands on the floor of the Louisiana legislature and shouts that if they take away Black people's right to vote, they are fools — and his enemies call him insane
A Cajun man hears his grandson speak English as his first language and realizes that something irreplaceable is being lost
An offshore oil worker watches the Gulf sunrise from a drilling platform fifty miles from shore and wonders if his family in Lafayette can see the same sky

key_events: New Orleans public school integration continues amid white boycotts and mob violence (1960–1962)
Civil Rights Act of 1964 outlaws segregation in public accommodations
Voting Rights Act of 1965 restores Black voting rights — Louisiana's Black voter registration surges
CORE (Congress of Racial Equality) organizes Freedom Rides through Louisiana (1961)
Deacons for Defense and Justice — armed Black self-defense group — organize in Jonesboro and Bogalusa (1964–1965)
Hurricane Betsy devastates New Orleans and southeastern Louisiana (September 9–10, 1965) — 76 killed, massive flooding
Offshore oil industry becomes the state's dominant economic force
Louisiana Cajun cultural revival begins — CODOFIL (Council for the Development of French in Louisiana) established (1968)
1960s.economic_conditions: The 1960s solidified oil and gas as Louisiana's economic engine. Offshore drilling expanded dramatically, with Louisiana supplying a significant share of U.S. petroleum production. Petrochemical plants proliferated along the Mississippi River. Tourism became increasingly important to New Orleans's economy. Agriculture — sugar, rice, soybeans — remained significant but employed fewer people as mechanization advanced. The civil rights movement's success in dismantling legal segregation began slowly opening economic opportunities for Black Louisianians. Federal poverty programs (War on Poverty) targeted Louisiana's poorest parishes.
dominant_industries: oil and gas (dominant — onshore and offshore)
1960s.migration_patterns: Black out-migration slowed as civil rights gains made Louisiana marginally more livable. Some Black professionals began returning. The oil industry continued attracting workers from across the region. Suburban growth around New Orleans, Baton Rouge, and Lafayette continued. Hurricane Betsy (1965) displaced tens of thousands in the New Orleans area, foreshadowing the catastrophe of Katrina forty years later. Cajun cultural revival efforts began reversing the assimilationist tide — French language education was encouraged for the first time in decades.
1960s.social_context: The civil rights movement transformed Louisiana. New Orleans's school integration, the Freedom Rides, sit-ins, and voter registration drives broke down legal segregation. The Deacons for Defense — armed Black men who protected civil rights workers in small Louisiana towns — represented a lesser-known but crucial part of the movement. The Voting Rights Act's impact was immediate and dramatic in Louisiana. Yet resistance was fierce: Klan violence, economic retaliation against Black activists, and deeply entrenched racial attitudes persisted. The Cajun cultural revival movement — sparked partly by shame at how Cajun identity had been suppressed — began reclaiming French language and Cajun pride.
1960s.daily_life_snapshot: In New Orleans, the 1960s brought the civil rights revolution and Hurricane Betsy in quick succession. Black Louisianians exercised rights long denied — voting, eating at formerly whites-only restaurants, attending desegregated schools (though white flight created de facto segregation). The French Quarter became a countercultural center and tourist magnet. In Cajun country, the oil industry brought prosperity and cultural disruption — young Cajuns worked on rigs and spoke English, while their grandparents struggled with the new world. In Black Creole communities, zydeco music flourished — Clifton Chenier, the 'King of Zydeco,' embodied the fusion of Cajun, Creole, blues, and R&B traditions.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Hurricane Betsy devastates New Orleans and southeast Louisiana (September 1965) — 76 killed, $1.42 billion in damage
Continued racial violence: Klan bombings, beatings, and murders
White flight from New Orleans public schools undermines integration
Environmental degradation from petrochemical industry — 'Cancer Alley' impacts grow

narrative_hooks: A Black voter in rural Louisiana walks into a registrar's office under the protection of the Voting Rights Act and registers for the first time — her grandmother tried fifty years ago and was turned away
